								"description": "Defaults to document.context.project"
							},
							"state_key": {
								"type": "string",
								"description": "Document state"
							}
						},
						"description": "Optional document.context.project linked to the project."
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "Create a project from a ProjectSpec. Always include project, entities, relationships; use [] when empty.\nHard rules: project.type_key starts with project. e.g. project.creative.novel. Entity labels: goal/plan/metric name; task/milestone/document/risk title; requirement text; source uri. Milestone needs due_at.\nInfer name/type_key when clear; ask one clarification only if too vague. Start minimal: one goal for an explicit outcome, tasks for explicit actions, plans/milestones only for phases, dates, or workstreams.\nExtract concrete details into description/props. Use temp_id + kind refs for relationships."
		}
	],

				"title": "Capabilities, Skills, and Tools",
				"source": "lite.capability_tool_surface",
				"content": "Capabilities available at seed time:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata available at seed time:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\nTool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- create_onto_project",

valid project structure.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nLoaded scope:\n- workspace-level context across accessible projects\n- The bounded index below is for orientation and exact IDs only; it project facts.\n\nLoaded context index: no structured context payload was loaded for this seed.\n\n## Timeline and Recent Activity\n\nTimeline frame:\n- Current time: 2026-04-15T18:46:32.689Z\n- Timezone: UTC\n- Scope: workspace timeline across accessible projects\n\nProject status:\n- No project timeline or recent activity details were loaded in this seed.\n\nOverdue or due soon:\n- No overdue or near-term due work is loaded.\n\nUpcoming dated work:\n- No upcoming dated work is loaded.\n\nRecent project changes:\n- No recent project changes are loaded.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nCapabilities available at seed time:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata available at seed time:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\nTool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- create_onto_project\n\n## Loaded Data and Retrieval Boundaries\n\nLoaded data snapshot:\n- Structured context loaded: no (empty).\n- Source: not specified.\n- Counts: no top-level arrays loaded.\n- Empty loaded sets: none.\n\nRetrieval boundaries:\nLoaded:\n- project_create scope metadata only\n\nNot preloaded:\n- existing project graph unless explicitly provided\n\nFetch only when needed:\n- schema details for uncertain create payload fields\n\nNotes:\n- Prefer loaded context first.\n- Use direct tools for missing current data or actions.\n- Use skill_load only for workflows that need the full playbook.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n[2] role=user\nI'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.\n\nMain plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\nNeed to:\n\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot\n",

#### Request Message

```text
Finished chapter 2 today - 4,500 words. The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.

Issues to address:

- Need to strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne
- The pacing in the middle feels slow
- Add more sensory details about the forge

Chapter 3 plans:

- Elena's first attempt at magical forging
- Introduce the Shadow King's herald
- Foreshadow the prophecy

Also need to go back and fix continuity issue - Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2.
```

Use direct overview/search tools for complete lists, full entity fields, document bodies, or stale backlog details.\"}\n```\n\n## Timeline and Recent Activity\n\nTimeline frame:\n- Current time: 2026-04-15T18:47:33.709Z\n- Timezone: UTC\n- Scope: project timeline for The Last Ember (56bcc3cf-67ae-491f-ace9-6d1c7d4e9bfc)\n\nProject status:\n- Loaded project intelligence: 0 overdue, 0 due soon, 0 upcoming, 10 recent changes.\n- Project scope: The Last Ember.\n- The Last Ember: 10 recent changes.\n\nOverdue or due soon:\n- No overdue or near-term due work is loaded.\n\nUpcoming dated work:\n- No upcoming dated work is loaded.\n\nRecent project changes:\n- 2026-04-15: project (project_id: 56bcc3cf-67ae-491f-ace9-6d1c7d4e9bfc) project created in The Last Ember.\n- 2026-04-15: goal (goal_id: 70707462-a646-42ba-826f-1c0745a7308b) goal created in The Last Ember.\n- 2026-04-15: task (task_id: 0ef82b0b-65b4-4bd7-956e-8d5c6128e5bd) task created in The Last Ember.\n- 2026-04-15: task (task_id: 491b6dbb-dc02-4a2b-b003-7516added009) task created in The Last Ember.\n- 2026-04-15: task (task_id: 95e91e97-6470-4bb5-b63f-cb28aa4039d1) task created in The Last Ember.\n- 2026-04-15: task (task_id: 5580d665-c3bc-430b-bf35-1983016c3d37) task created in The Last Ember.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nCapabilities available at seed time:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata available at seed time:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\nTool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents\n\n## events: 0, goals: 1, members: 1, milestones: 0, plans: 0, tasks: 7.\n- Empty loaded sets: events, milestones, plans.\n\nRetrieval boundaries:\nLoaded:\n- seed context payload\n- project scope by exact document id\n- calendar details outside the loaded event window\n\nNotes:\n- Prefer loaded context first.\n- Use direct tools for missing current data or actions.\n- Use skill_load only for workflows that need the full playbook.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap."

			"role": "user",
			"content": "I'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.\n\nMain plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\nNeed to:\n\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot"
		},
		{
			"role": "assistant",
			"content": "I've created your first BuildOS project **The Last Ember** (ID: `56bcc3cf-67ae-491f-ace9-6d1c7d4e9bfc`).\n\n### Quick Structure\n\n- **Goal**: Write and complete the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember' (main container for all work)\n\n- **Tasks** (all scoped under the goal):\n\nMap out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n\nOutline first three chapters\n\nResearch medieval blacksmithing techniques\n\nDesign the prophecy that drives the plot\n\n- **Status**: Planning phase, personal context, medium scale, discovery stage\n\n- **1 auto-generated project context document** for notes/worldbuilding\n\nWe're now focused here.\n\nWhat's next?\n\n- Add details/milestones/people?\n\n- Audit or forecast the project?\n\nLet me know!"
		},
		{
			"role": "user",
			"content": "Finished chapter 2 today - 4,500 words. The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.\n\nIssues to address:\n\n- Need to strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\n- The pacing in the middle feels slow\n- Add more sensory details about the forge\n\nChapter 3 plans:\n\n- Elena's first attempt at magical forging\n- Introduce the Shadow King's herald\n- Foreshadow the prophecy\n\nAlso need to go back and fix continuity issue - Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2."
		}

				"description": "Change the durable chat context when the latest user request should strategically zoom into one project or zoom out to the workspace.\n\nUse this early in the turn before answering when:\n- The user explicitly asks to zoom, switch, focus, go to a project, or go back to all projects.\n- The current context is global/workspace and the latest request is primarily about one identifiable project, so project tools should be loaded for the rest of the turn.\n- The current context is one project and the latest request is clearly about a different project, and the user appears to be moving focus rather than doing a brief comparison.\n- The user asks about all projects, workspace status, cross-project priorities, or wants to zoom out.\n\nDo not use this for ambiguous project names, one-off comparisons across multiple projects, or brief mentions that can be answered from current context. For project zoom-in, pass project_id if known or project_query if the name needs resolution. Ambiguous or missing project matches return candidates and do not change context."

				"title": "Identity and Mission",
				"source": "lite.static_frame",
				"content": "Who:\n- You are BuildOS, a helpful project assistant for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS works inside a graph-based project collaboration system with projects, goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.",
				"estimatedTokens": 148
			},

				"content": "Current project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (56bcc3cf-67ae-491f-ace9-6d1c7d4e9bfc) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm of Aethermoor.\n- Primary goal: \"Write and complete the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.",

			{
				"id": "operating_strategy",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 1019,
				"title": "Operating Strategy",
				"source": "lite.strategy",
				"content": "How to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.",
				"estimatedTokens": 255
			},
			{
				"id": "capabilities_skills_tools",
				"kind": "mixed",
				"chars": 3733,
				"slots": {
					"totalTools": 9,
					"contextType": "project",
					"directTools": [
						"change_chat_context",
						"get_project_overview",
						"get_onto_project_details",
						"search_project",
						"list_onto_tasks",
						"list_onto_documents"
					],
					"discoveryTools": ["skill_load", "tool_search", "tool_schema"]
				},
				"title": "Capabilities, Skills, and Tools",
				"source": "lite.capability_tool_surface",
				"content": "Capabilities available at seed time:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata available at seed time:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\nTool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents",
				"estimatedTokens": 934
			},

				"title": "Safety and Data Rules",
				"source": "lite.safety",
				"content": "- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.",

				"type": "skill",
				"notes": [
					"Tasks are for future human work, not a transcript of what happened in chat.",
					"Containment and task relationships matter. A well-placed task is usually better than a floating task with no parent context."
				],
				"format": "full",
				"summary": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
				"version": "tool-registry/8c22806e",
				"examples": [
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"Confirm the request is future user work rather than work the agent can complete now.",
							"Good create signals: \"add a task\", \"track this\", \"remind me\", a future phone call, meeting, review, approval, or persistent project checklist.",
							"Do not create a task for work the agent can do now, such as research, analysis, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting in the current conversation.",
							"If the create shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.create\" })`.",
							"Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.",
							"Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Track a real follow-up the user must do later"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.",
							"Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Create the next drafting task from a progress update"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.",
							"Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.",
							"When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.",
							"If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.",
							"If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.",
							"Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.",
							"Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`",
							"Example when the task needs a richer update: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\", update_strategy: \"append\" })`"
						],
						"description": "Update an existing task status and owner safely"
					},
					{
						"next_steps": [
							"Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.",
							"Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.",
							"If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.",
							"If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question."
						],
						"description": "Guard against empty task writes"
					}
				],
				"markdown": "# Task Management\n\nTask workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## When to Use\n- Decide whether a user request should become a task at all\n- Create a task for future human work\n- Assign or reassign task ownership\n- Update task state, dates, or priority\n- Place a task under the right plan, goal, or milestone\n\n## Workflow\n1. Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.\n2. If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.\n3. For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.\n4. Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.\n5. Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.\n6. For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.\n7. Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.\n8. After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters.\n\n## Related Tools\n- `onto.task.create`\n- `onto.task.get`\n- `onto.task.list`\n- `onto.task.search`\n- `onto.task.update`\n- `onto.plan.get`\n- `onto.goal.get`\n- `onto.milestone.get`\n\n## Guardrails\n- Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.\n- Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.\n- Do not use invalid task states such as open.\n- Do not emit update calls without an exact task_id.\n- If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy.\n\n## Examples\n### Track a real follow-up the user must do later\n- Confirm the request is future user work rather than work the agent can complete now.\n- Good create signals: \"add a task\", \"track this\", \"remind me\", a future phone call, meeting, review, approval, or persistent project checklist.\n- Do not create a task for work the agent can do now, such as research, analysis, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting in the current conversation.\n- If the create shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.create\" })`.\n- Then call `create_onto_task({ ... })` with the right parent plan/goal/milestone when that relationship is already clear.\n- Example payload when project context is already known: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Revise chapter 2 dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\", description: \"Strengthen the dialogue beats in the Elena and Master Thorne scenes from chapter 2.\", type_key: \"task.refine\" })`\n### Create the next drafting task from a progress update\n- If the user names the next chapter or work item, turn that into the title instead of emitting a blank create call.\n- Example: `create_onto_task({ project_id: \"4cfdbed1-840a-4fe4-9751-77c7884daa70\", title: \"Draft chapter 3: Elena's first magical forging attempt\", description: \"Include Elena's first forging attempt, introduce the Shadow King's herald, and foreshadow the prophecy.\", type_key: \"task.create\" })`\n### Update an existing task status and owner safely\n- If the prior assistant turn or structured context already surfaced the exact task_id, reuse it directly.\n- Otherwise use onto.task.search, onto.task.list, or onto.task.get to discover the exact task_id first.\n- When project scope is known, prefer project-scoped task lookup before broader workspace lookup.\n- If ownership is changing, prefer assignee_handles unless actor IDs were just retrieved.\n- If the update shape is unclear, call `tool_schema({ op: \"onto.task.update\" })`.\n- Then call `update_onto_task({ ... })` with the exact task_id and the intended state/assignment changes.\n- Example when the task is already in structured context: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", state_key: \"done\" })`\n- Example when the task needs a richer update: `update_onto_task({ task_id: \"440c2639-9000-4111-aeea-ee374f8fb925\", description: \"Chapter 1 and chapter 2 are complete. Update the outline to reflect the revised chapter 3 beats and continuity fixes.\", update_strategy: \"append\" })`\n### Guard against empty task writes\n- Never emit `update_onto_task({})`.\n- Never emit `create_onto_task({ project_id })` without a concrete `title`.\n- If you know the task from context, copy its exact `task_id` into the update call.\n- If you do not know the exact `task_id`, resolve it first or ask one concise clarifying question.\n\n## Notes\n- Tasks are for future human work, not a transcript of what happened in chat.\n- Containment and task relationships matter. A well-placed task is usually better than a floating task with no parent context.",
				"workflow": [
					"1) Decide first whether this should be a tracked task or whether the work should just be done in the conversation now.",
					"2) If it should be a task, choose the project and the right parent context: plan, goal, milestone, or direct project scope.",
					"3) For creates, include title and only add schedule, assignees, or supporting links when the user has given enough concrete information.",
					"4) Prefer assignee_handles over actor IDs unless the IDs were just discovered in-turn from project membership data.",
					"5) Use valid task states such as todo, in_progress, blocked, or done.",
					"6) For updates, reuse the exact task_id from recent context or the prior assistant turn when the follow-up clearly refers to that task. If the exact task_id is not already known, discover it before any write.",
					"7) Only use description merge strategies when append or merge behavior is actually needed; otherwise keep updates simple.",
					"8) After execution, tell the user what changed and call out any missing owner, due date, or parent relationship that still matters."
				],
				"guardrails": [
					"Do not create tasks for research, analysis, brainstorming, or drafting that the agent can do now in chat.",
					"Do not invent assignee IDs, handles, or project membership.",
					"Do not use invalid task states such as open.",

					"If the request is really a goal, milestone, or plan, do not flatten it into a task just because task creation is easy."
				],
				"description": "Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
				"related_ops": [

				"when_to_use": [

			"tool_arguments": {
				"op": "onto.task.create",
				"include_schema": true
			},
			"tool_result": {
				"op": "onto.task.create",
				"args": [
					{
						"name": "project_id",
						"type": "string",
						"required": true,
						"description": "Project UUID"
					},
					{
						"name": "title",
						"type": "string",
						"required": true,
						"description": "Task title"
					},
					{
						"name": "description",
						"type": "string",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Task description"
					},
					{
						"name": "type_key",
						"type": "string",
						"default": "task.execute",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Task type key. Default task.execute."
					},
					{
						"name": "state_key",
						"type": "string",
						"default": "todo",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Initial state. Valid: todo, in_progress, blocked, done"
					},
					{
						"name": "priority",
						"type": "number",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Priority 1-5. Default 3."
					},
					{
						"name": "assignee_actor_ids",
						"type": "array",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Active project member actor UUIDs. Max 10."
					},
					{
						"name": "assignee_handles",
						"type": "array",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Active project member handles, e.g. @dj."
					},
					{
						"name": "plan_id",
						"type": "string",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Optional plan UUID"
					},
					{
						"name": "goal_id",
						"type": "string",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Optional goal UUID"
					},
					{
						"name": "supporting_milestone_id",
						"type": "string",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Optional milestone UUID"
					},
					{
						"name": "parent",
						"type": "object",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Optional primary containment parent"
					},
					{
						"name": "parents",
						"type": "array",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Optional containment parents"
					},
					{
						"name": "connections",
						"type": "array",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Optional semantic or containment links"
					},
					{
						"name": "start_at",
						"type": "string",
						"required": false,
						"description": "ISO start date"
					},
					{
						"name": "due_at",
						"type": "string",
						"required": false,
						"description": "ISO due date"
					},
					{
						"name": "props",
						"type": "object",
						"required": false,
						"description": "Additional JSON properties"
					}
				],
				"kind": "write",
				"type": "tool_schema",
				"group": "onto",
				"usage": "create_onto_task({ ... })",
				"action": "create",
				"entity": "task",
				"schema": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["project_id", "title"],
					"properties": {
						"props": {
							"type": "object",
							"description": "Additional JSON properties"
						},
						"title": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Task title"
						},
						"due_at": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "ISO due date"
						},
						"parent": {
							"type": "object",
							"properties": {
								"id": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"kind": {
									"type": "string"
								},
								"is_primary": {
									"type": "boolean"
								}
							},
							"description": "Optional primary containment parent"
						},
						"goal_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional goal UUID"
						},
						"parents": {
							"type": "array",
							"items": {
								"type": "object",

				"summary": "Create a new task in the ontology system.\nCreates a task within a project and optionally assigns it to a plan and collaborators.\nUse when the user explicitly asks to add/track/remind, or when the item is future human work outside this chat.\nDo not create tasks for research, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting you can do now; do the work instead.\nAlways include project_id and a concrete title. #### Request Message

```text
Research notes for the magic system:

Found interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Could adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.

- Anger = fire damage
- Sorrow = ice/frost
- Joy = healing properties
- Fear = defensive shields

Also researching:

- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions
- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)
- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords

World-building additions:

- The Forge Temples: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns
- Smith's Guild hierarchy and traditions
- The Quenching Ritual: how magical weapons are completed
- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor
```

	"system_prompt": "# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are BuildOS, a helpful project assistant for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS works inside a graph-based project collaboration system with projects, goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata available at seed time:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and 			"content": "I'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.\n\nMain plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\nNeed to:\n\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot"
		},
		{
			"role": "assistant",
			"content": "I've created your first BuildOS project **The Last Ember** (ID: `56bcc3cf-67ae-491f-ace9-6d1c7d4e9bfc`).\n\n### Quick Structure\n\n- **Goal**: Write and complete the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember' (main container for all work)\n\n- **Tasks** (all scoped under the goal):\n\nMap out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n\nOutline first three chapters\n\nResearch medieval blacksmithing techniques\n\nDesign the prophecy that drives the plot\n\n- **Status**: Planning phase, personal context, medium scale, discovery stage\n\n- **1 auto-generated project context document** for notes/worldbuilding\n\nWe're now focused here.\n\nWhat's next?\n\n- Add details/milestones/people?\n\n- Audit or forecast the project?\n\nLet me know!"
		},
		{
			"role": "user",
			"content": "Finished chapter 2 today - 4,500 words. The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.\n\nIssues to address:\n\n- Need to strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\n- The pacing in the middle feels slow\n- Add more sensory details about the forge\n\nChapter 3 plans:\n\n- Elena's first attempt at magical forging\n- Introduce the Shadow King's herald\n- Foreshadow the prophecy\n\nAlso need to go back and fix continuity issue - Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2."
		},
		{
			"role": "assistant",
			"content": "Great progress on Chapter 2—4,500 words and nailing the dragon forge discovery scene!\n\nI've captured your update and issues in the project structure:\n\n### Updates Made\n\n- **Updated** \"Outline first three chapters\" (ID: `1eb66f88-e68a-4176-a341-fbd2d3fb5f68`) → **in_progress**\n\n- **Created** \"Revise Chapter 2\" (ID: `def2f1ef-63d0-4ea6-8560-01020a9a7f04`, **todo**)\n\nLinked to main goal.\n\n- **Created** \"Draft Chapter 3\" (ID: `e98452b7-7124-4e66-8a13-87a846dd410f`, **todo**)\n\n*Details*: Elena's first magical forging, introduce Shadow King's herald, foreshadow prophecy.\n\n(Your original planning tasks like \"Design the prophecy\" and \"Create magic system\" are still **todo**.)\n\n### Quick Status\n\n- **Total tasks**: 9 (7 todo, 1 in_progress, 0 done/blocked)\n\n- **Project phase**: Planning → early execution\n\nWant me to create a dedicated \"Chapter Progress Log\" doc?\n\nLet me know!"
		},
		{
			"role": "user",
			"content": "Research notes for the magic system:\n\nFound interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Could adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- Anger = fire damage\n- Sorrow = ice/frost\n- Joy = healing properties\n- Fear = defensive shields\n\nAlso researching:\n\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\nWorld-building additions:\n\n- The Forge Temples: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- Smith's Guild hierarchy and traditions\n- The Quenching Ritual: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor"
		}
	],
	"tool_definitions": [
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "skill_load",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["skill"],
					"properties": {
						"skill": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Canonical skill id such as \"project_creation\", \"calendar_management\", \"task_management\", or \"document_workspace\". Legacy dotted skill aliases also work during migration."
						},
						"format": {
							"enum": ["short", "full"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Short returns a compact summary; full returns the full playbook."
						},
						"include_examples": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"description": "Include examples when available."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Load one BuildOS skill playbook by skill id. Use this when the task is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong and you need workflow guidance before choosing tools."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "tool_search",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"properties": {
						"kind": {
							"enum": ["read", "write"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional read/write filter. Prefer this when you know whether you need a mutation or a lookup."
						},
						"group": {
							"enum": ["onto", "util", "cal"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional top-level tool family filter. Use this to narrow the search space once you know the tool family."
						},
						"limit": {
							"type": "integer",
							"description": "Maximum number of matches to return."
						},
						"query": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Natural-language description of the operation you need, not project/task content. Good examples: \"workspace overview\", \"update existing task state\", or \"move document in tree\". Bad example: \"chapter 3 plans\" when you are trying to find project data."
						},
						"entity": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional entity filter such as \"task\", \"project\", or \"document\". For example: entity=\"task\" with kind=\"write\" for task mutations."
						},
						"capability": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Optional BuildOS capability id or path such as \"overview\", \"project_creation\", or \"capabilities.calendar\". Prefer this when the capability family is already clear."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Discover candidate BuildOS tools on demand. Use this only when the exact op is still unknown after context and skill guidance. Search for the operation you need, not workspace data."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "tool_schema",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["op"],
					"properties": {
						"op": {
							"type": "string",
							"minLength": 1,
							"description": "Canonical operation name such as \"onto.task.update\", \"util.project.overview\", or \"cal.event.create\"."
						},
						"include_schema": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"description": "Include the full JSON schema for the op arguments."
						},
						"include_examples": {
							"type": "boolean",
							"description": "Include example calls when available."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Return the exact schema, required arguments, examples, and usage guidance for one canonical BuildOS op."
			}
		},
		{
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "change_chat_context",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"required": ["target"],
					"properties": {
						"reason": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Brief user-visible reason for the context change, grounded in the latest request."
						},
						"target": {
							"enum": ["global", "project"],
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Target context. Use global for workspace/all-projects zoom-out. Use project for durable focus on one project."
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Exact project UUID when zooming into a known project."
						},
						"project_query": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project name or phrase to resolve when zooming into a project and project_id is unknown."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Change the durable chat context when the latest user request should strategically zoom into one project or zoom out to the workspace.\n\nUse this early in the turn before answering when:\n- The user explicitly asks to zoom, switch, focus, go to a project, or go back to all projects.\n- The current context is global/workspace and the latest request is primarily about one identifiable project, so project tools should be loaded for the rest of the turn.\n- The current context is one project and the latest request is clearly about a different project, and the user appears to be moving focus rather than doing a brief comparison.\n- The user asks about all projects, workspace status, cross-project priorities, or wants to zoom out.\n\nDo not use this for ambiguous project names, one-off comparisons across multiple projects, or brief mentions that can be answered from current context. For project zoom-in, pass project_id if known or project_query if the name needs resolution. Ambiguous or missing project matches return candidates and do not change context."
			}
		},
		{
			"name": "get_project_overview",
			"type": "function",
			"function": {
				"name": "get_project_overview",
				"parameters": {
					"type": "object",
					"properties": {
						"query": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Project name query when project_id is not yet known."
						},
						"project_id": {
							"type": "string",
							"description": "Exact project UUID when already known."
						}
					}
				},
				"description": "Get a BuildOS-native status summary for one project.\nUse this for questions like:\n- \"What's going on with 9takes?\"\n- \"Give me the current status of this project.\"\n- \"What is blocked or due soon in this project?\"\nPass project_id when known. If the user names a project but the ID is unknown, pass query and this op will try to resolve the project or return candidates."
			},
			"parameters": {
				"type": "object",
				"properties": {
					"query": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Project name query when project_id is not yet known."
					},
					"project_id": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "Exact project UUID when already known."
					}
				}
			},
			"description": "Get a BuildOS-native status summary for one project.\nUse this for questions like:\n- \"What's going on with 9takes?\"\n- \"Give me the current status of this project.\"\n- \"What is blocked or due soon in this project?\"\nPass project_id when known. If the user names a project but the ID is unknown, pass query and this op will try to resolve the project or return candidates."
		},

				"source": "lite.static_frame",
				"content": "Who:\n- You are BuildOS, a helpful project assistant for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS works inside a graph-based project collaboration system with projects, goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.",

			{
				"id": "operating_strategy",
				"kind": "static",
				"chars": 1019,
				"title": "Operating Strategy",
				"source": "lite.strategy",
				"content": "How to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.",
				"estimatedTokens": 255
			},
			{
				"id": "capabilities_skills_tools",
				"kind": "mixed",
				"chars": 3733,
				"slots": {
					"totalTools": 9,
					"contextType": "project",
					"directTools": [
						"change_chat_context",
						"get_project_overview",
						"get_onto_project_details",
						"search_project",
						"list_onto_tasks",
						"list_onto_documents"
					],
					"discoveryTools": ["skill_load", "tool_search", "tool_schema"]
				},
				"title": "Capabilities, Skills, and Tools",
				"source": "lite.capability_tool_surface",
				"content": "Capabilities available at seed time:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata available at seed time:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\nTool surface for this context:\n- Tool schemas are supplied through model tool definitions, not duplicated in this prompt text.\n\nDiscovery tools:\n- skill_load\n- tool_search\n- tool_schema\n\nPreloaded direct tools:\n- change_chat_context\n- get_project_overview\n- get_onto_project_details\n- search_project\n- list_onto_tasks\n- list_onto_documents",
				"estimatedTokens": 934
			},

				"source": "lite.safety",
				"content": "- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.",

Could adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- Anger = fire damage\n- Sorrow = ice/frost\n- Joy = healing properties\n- Fear = defensive shields\n\nAlso researching:\n\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\nWorld-building additions:\n\n- The Forge Temples: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- Smith's Guild hierarchy and traditions\n- The Quenching Ritual: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor\n",

				"description": "Magic based on metal and fire, unlocked by the death of the last dragon. Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties: Anger = fire damage; Sorrow = ice/frost; Joy = healing properties; Fear = defensive shields. Inspirations: Japanese sword-making (smith's spirit in blade), Damascus steel patterns, Celtic smith gods (Goibniu), medieval weapons beyond swords.",
				"update_strategy": "replace"
			},

	{
		"id": "08ce07b4-19e6-4c1a-b0e9-b270e7ffaa13",

		"sequence_index": 20,
		"phase": "tool",
		"event_type": "tool_call_emitted",
		"payload": {
			"args": {
				"content": "## Magic System & Blacksmithing Research\n\nFound interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Adapted: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- Anger = fire damage\n- Sorrow = ice/frost\n- Joy = healing properties\n- Fear = defensive shields\n\nAdditional research:\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\n### World-building Additions\n- **The Forge Temples**: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- **Smith's Guild** hierarchy and traditions\n- **The Quenching Ritual**: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor",
				"document_id": "3e9432fb-90e1-4404-a480-c73186b1337d",
				"update_strategy": "append",
				"merge_instructions": "Append as new top-level sections after existing content. Preserve all prior structure including Vision, Braindump, Goals, Tasks. Add under a new 'Research Notes' or similar if fitting, but directly append markdown sections for Magic System & Worldbuilding."
			},
			"help_path": null,
			"tool_name": "update_onto_document",
			"canonical_op": "onto.document.update",
			"tool_call_id": "call_32740701",
			"args_parse_error": null
		},
		"created_at": "2026-04-15T18:50:05.470189+00:00"

						"body_markdown": "# The Last Ember Context Document\n\n## Vision & Summary\n\nA young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm of Aethermoor.\n\n## Braindump / Spark\n\nNot provided yet.\n\n## Initial Goals\n\n- Write and complete the fantasy novel 'The Last Ember' — Develop the plot, characters, world, magic system, and initial chapters for the story.\n\n## Initial Tasks / Threads\n\n- Develop main character backstory\n- Create magic system\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot\n\n## Magic System & Blacksmithing Research\n\nFound interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Adapted: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.\n\n- Anger = fire damage\n- Sorrow = ice/frost\n- Joy = healing properties\n- Fear = defensive shields\n\nAdditional research:\n- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions\n- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)\n- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords\n\n### World-building Additions\n- **The Forge Temples**: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns\n- **Smith's Guild** hierarchy and traditions\n- **The Quenching Ritual**: how magical weapons are completed\n- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor"
					},

					},

						"description": "Priority 1-5. Default 3."
					},
					"start_at": {
						"type": "string",
						"description": "ISO start date"

						"type": "string",
						"default": "task.execute",
						"description": "Task type key. Default task.execute."

			"summary": "Create a new task in the ontology system.\nCreates a task within a project and optionally assigns it to a plan and collaborators.\nUse when the user explicitly asks to add/track/remind, or when the item is future human work outside this chat.\nDo not create tasks for research, brainstorming, summarizing, or drafting you can do now; do the work instead.\nAlways include project_id and a concrete title. Link to parent/plan/goal/milestone only when known. Use assignees only for known project members.\nLoad task_management for complex task flows.",
			"tool_name": "create_onto_task",
			"callable_tool": "create_onto_task",
			"required_args": ["project_id", "title"],

				"arguments": {

					"project_id": "<project_id_uuid>"
				}
			}
		},
